---
title: "Expert Report for the Royal Commission on Antisemitism and Social Cohesion"
author: "Daniel Allington"
date: "2026-04-18"
output:
  word_document: default
  html_document: default
bibliography: "royal-commission.bib"
documentclass: article
csl: "chicago-fullnote-mod.csl"
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE)

library(dplyr)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
```

# What is antisemitism?
In its everyday sense, the word 'antisemitism' simply means '[p]rejudice, hostility, or discrimination against Jewish people' [@oed_antisemitism_2025, sense 1]. However, scholars often use the word in a more specific sense, refering not to 'th[e] entire history [of anti-Jewish _praxis_], from its earliest beginnings in classical and pagan times through to the present', but rather to the bigotry and persecution faced by Jews in Europe and its diaspora during a specific historical period which 'beg[an] sometime in the latter part of the nineteenth century' [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 12]. 

This was indeed the period during which the German word, _Antisemitismus_ --- of which 'antisemitism' is the English translation --- was coined by Wilhelm Marr: a left-wing radical, strongly influenced by anarchism. Unlike Christian Jew-haters, who took issue above all with the religion of Judaism, Marr --- an atheist --- was also opposed to Jewish _cultural_ influence on Germany, which he saw as a form of 'foreign domination' [@musiedlak_wilhelm_2021, p. 83-84]. Marr understood the people of Europe to be divided into three 'races', which he dubbed the Slavic, the Latin, and the Germanic [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 66]. This terminology was linguistic in origin, these being the names of the transnational families into which fell the languages spoken by members of these supposed 'races'. To emphasise the non-European origins of the Jewish people, Marr initially referred to Jews as 'Asian', using the word 'Asianism' to describe the political system by which they had come --- in his eyes --- to dominate Europe [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 66-68]. However, by the time of his 1879 pamphlet, _The Victory of Judaism over Germanism_, he had come to refer to Jews as 'Semitic' --- this being the name of the language family to which the Jewish language of Hebrew belonged --- and to use the word 'Semitism' to describe the cultural and economic practices by which they had --- in his view --- secretly conquered Europe, from the time of the Emperor Titus onwards:

> Foreign rule has been thrust upon us. 1800 years lasted the battle against Jewish domination, which hardly ever strayed from its biblical tradition. ... Starting from modest beginnings, [the Semitic people] outgrew you, it corrupted society in all of its aspects, squeezed all idealism out of it, occupies the most controlling influence in trade and daily life, penetrates ever more into public office, controls the theatre, forms a social-political front, and has left almost nothing for you, except raw labor which it itself has always shunned; it has tranformed talent into shiny virtuosity, pimpish advertising into the goddess of public opinion and --- rules you today.

> ...

> We harbor a resilient, tough, intelligent, foreign tribe among us, who knows how to take advantage of every form of abstract reality. It is not the individual Jew, it is Jewish spirit and Jewish awareness which have taken over the world. ... 

> These are all historical-cultural facts, so unique in their aspects, so  awesome, that it is impossible to dispute them using everyday polemics. The proud Roman Empire has not been able to win such triumphs with all the might of its arms, as has typical Semitism won in the Occident and particularly in Germany. [@marr_victory_1879_2009, p. 23-24]

Marr was far from being the first writer to imagine the hidden hand of an international Jewish conspiracy behind every bad thing in the world. It was an idea which had already found influential expression in the 1868 novel _Biarritz_, by Hermann Goedsche (writing as 'Sir John Retcliffe'), from which Tsarist agents would later plagiarise much of the content of _The Protocols of the Elders of Zion_: a literary forgery falsely presented as the record of a speech given by Theodor Herzl at the First Zionist Congress [For the classic study, see @cohn_warrant_1967]. However, Marr was the first writer to transform this conspiracy fantasy into a rallying cry for what his biographer called 'a war against the Jews' [@zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 78]. Because 'Semitism' was by this point Marr's preferred word for Jewishness, he naturally used the word 'Antisemitism' to describe the political programme that he laid out from 1879 onwards. Although Marr himself fell into obscurity, that programme provided the ideological foundation for National Socialism, or Nazism.

Marr was happy to identify as an antisemite, but many later adherents of his programme preferred to refer to it by alternative names. Indeed, the 1944 edition of Theodor Fritsch's _Handbook of the Jewish Question_ --- by that point a standard Nazi text --- rejected the term 'antisemitism' as 'unsuitable, ... for the reason that there are other peoples with Semitic languages, such as the Arabs, who stand in complete opposition to the Jews' [Quoted in @zimmermann_wilhelm_1986, p. 113-115]. Nonetheless, the name stuck, and moreover broadened in meaning, such that it not only continued to denote the specific anti-Jewish politics of Marr and the Nazis, but also became the usual word by which to refer to _any_ form of anti-Jewish action, sentiment, or ideation, whilst at the same time continuing to denote --- for many scholars of the field --- the ideological anti-Jewishness which arose in 19th Century Europe.

Although the above may strike the hasty reader as no more than an academic exercise in splitting hairs, this background is vitally important in order to make sense of key scholarly debates on antisemitism --- debates to which this report will be returning below. Moreover, it provides a helpful introduction to antisemitism as it exists today, and to what makes it such a unique form of hatred.

This is because, even if we are to use the word 'antisemitism' in its everyday sense, i.e. as a synonym of 'Jew-hate', the specific form of hate that is typically directed at Jews has been shaped on a very deep level by political history. Antisemitism --- in this sense --- is not simply a matter of dislike or ill-feeling towards a particular ethno-religious group, but of dislike and ill-feeling founded on a mythology that has built up over many centuries, with distinct and enduring contributions from different cultures at different times. Moreover, this mythology does not simply concern the alleged actions or characteristics of the members of that ethno-religious group. Rather, it purports to explain the history and present condition of the _entire world_, according Jews and their alleged crimes a central but villainous role in the story of the human race. This is why crimes which target Jews --- whether carried out by Nazis, Islamists, or anyone else --- have so often taken a form which is incomprehensible to many people today: the criminals typically see themselves not as lashing out against innocent and often defenceless individuals, but as striking a blow against an almost cosmic evil whose destruction is necessary for the redemption of the world as a whole. For example, an official Nazi Party publication declared in 1937 that 'Jewry's ruthless drive for world domination was stopped only by National Socialism' [Quoted in @bytwerk_believing_2015, p. 214].

## Christian antisemitism (delete heading)

## Muslim antisemitism (delete heading)

## Recent examples (delete heading)

The historically complex nature of Jew-hate can be seen from a brief consideration of recent crimes in Australia targeting Jews and locations associated with Jews. In 2024, the office of the then Attorney General, Mark Drefus was vandalised, with the words 'Who does AG Mark Dreyfus serve?' being painted on the pavement outside [@buckley_antisemitic_2025]. Although the _occasion_ of this attack was the Israel-Hamas war, it functioned by invoking the longstanding antisemitic trope of Jewish loyalty to supposed international Jewish interests (as seen in Marr's polemics, and also in more influential antisemitic works, such as the _Protocols of the Elders of Zion_). A few months later, the office of Josh Burns --- another Jewish politician --- was attacked by arsonists who spray-painted horns onto a photograph of Burns and added the slogan, 'Zionism is fascism' [@abc_activists_2024]. This act of vandalism combined pseudo-political discourse with 13th century European depictions of Jews as demonic horned figures [@bertman_antisemitic_2009]. Shortly afterwards, the Sydney suburb of Woollahra, known for its large Jewish minority, saw a number of arson attacks combined with anti-Israel graffiti [@buckley_antisemitic_2025, n.p.], while Mount Sinai College --- a Jewish school --- was vandalised not only with the message 'fuck the Jews' but also with the words 'killers' and 'merderers [sic]' [@buckley_antisemitic_2025, n.p.; @willan_heightened_2025, 00:15]: accusations which may have been occasioned by the Israel-Hamas war, but which were commonplace in Medieval Europe and Nazi Germany [See @trachtenberg_devil_1943].  

These examples (from which I have intentionally left out the massacre which prompted the official inquiry to which this report responds) illustrate two key points. The first is that contemporary acts of Jew-hate are saturated with historical baggage, and as such can only be understood by reference to the whole history of Jew-hate. The second is that, as at earlier stages in history, attacks on individual Jews are often rationalised in terms of the supposed evils of a Jewish or 'Zionist' collective. Whether we are to refer to this phenomenon as 'antisemitism' or as something else (and, for the sake of comprehensibility, this report will from this point onwards use the word 'antisemitism' in its ordinary sense, as defined by the Oxford English Dictionary at the beginning of this section), it is necessary to acknowledge these fundamental ideas.

This is, indeed, the only way in which it is possible to understand the statement issued by Iran-backed Islamist group Harakat Ashab al-Yamin al-Islamia on the occasion of the stabbing of two visibly Jewish men in London while this report was in preparation:

> Zionists were targeted by our lone wolves in the Golders Green area of London. The heroic act left two Zionists critically wounded and on the brink of death ...

> Historically, the Jews are the killers of Jesus Christ, and today, the Zionists are the killers of innocent women and children.

> We call upon all free people in the world, especially Muslims, to carry out qualitative operations in defense of humanity, occupied Palestine, al-Aqsa Mosque, and the prisoners. Furthermore, anyone who is able to kill Zionists should not hesitate. [Quoted in @starr_wounded_2026, n.p.]

How is it possible to identify knife attacks against random members of the British public, singled out on the basis of their religious clothing, as 'qualitative operations in defense of humanity'? It is possible because this has been the logic of antisemitism for centuries.

# Similarities and differences between antisemitism and other prejudices against racial, ethnic, and national groups
While any religious, ethnic, or national group may become the object of prejudice and undeserved hostility, antisemitism is unique in that it is founded on an ancient, but constantly evolving body of counter-factual belief concerning the world as a whole. The difference between antisemitism and other forms of group-based hate in this regard may be illustrated by example. Here, I have arbitrarily chosen the example of prejudice and hostility faced by Australians of Samoan origin.

Anti-Samoan bigotry is typically founded on a racist assumption that Samoans (and other indigenous Polynesians) are 'violent, unmotivated, and government-dependent' [@radclyffe_fob_2024, p. 1070]. This stereotype is very similar to stereotypes applied to many other immigrant groups, both in Australia and around the world. It would be extremely surprising to see anti-Samoan graffiti suggesting that Samoans are more loyal to Samoa than to Australia, or that Samoans are enemies of God, or that the Samoan government is fascist, or that the Samoan army kills non-Samoan children, or that Samoans and their supporters have an excessive influence on the Australian government --- and perhaps also the governments of other world powers. It would be still more surprising to see attacks on random Samoans justified as 'qualitative operations in defense of humanity'. The idea is bizarre, even verging on the ridiculous. Only Jews are spoken about in this way.

Where other minority groups are targeted, this is often on the basis of a barely-examined xenophobic tendency to see the majority community as superior. But where Jews are targeted, this is typically on the basis of a longstanding network of interlocking beliefs about world history and politics: beliefs which may play an analogous role to religious faith (as in the case of the atheist Wilhelm Marr) or which may in turn be deeply interlocked with religious faith (as in the case of the Christian and Muslim antisemites discussed above). Individuals and political movements are likely to go to much greater lengths in their attacks and persecutions if they see their target as irredeemably evil, supremely powerful, and a threat to the entire world, than if they are motivated simply by a small-minded dislike of people different from themselves. This is, ultimately, why Nazi Germany perpetrated the Holocaust, and why the Islamic Republic has forced the people of Iran into penury for the sake of an unwinnable proxy war against Israel.

None of this should be taken to imply that targeting of Jews is inherently _worse_ than the targeting of any other group: vandalism is vandalism, arson is arson, assault is assault, and murder is murder. What it means is that the targeting of Jews is motivated a peculiar way, and that this motivation is often very deeply ingrained, being strongly integrated with an individual's worldview, politics, and social identity. History shows us the extraordinary lengths to which Jew-haters have often been willing to go.

# Should governmental responses to antisemitism be based on a particular definition of antisemitic conduct?
Government responses to antisemitism should be based on an analysis of antisemitism. 

# The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance Working Definition of Antisemitism

The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance Working Definition of Antisemitism (henceforth, the IHRA Definition) defines antisemitism as 'a certain perception of Jews, which may be expressed as hatred toward Jews' [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 1].

The background to the IHRA definition.

> Antisemitic acts are criminal when they are so defined by law ...

> Criminal acts are antisemitic when the targets ... are selected because they are, or are perceived to be, Jewish or linked to Jews.

> Antisemitic discrimination is the denial to Jews of opportunities or services available to others, and is illegal in many countries. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 2]

International adoption of the IHRA definition by governments and institutions.

Use of the IHRA definition to determine whether conduct is or is not antisemitic.

Controversies arising in relation to the IHRA Definition and its examples.

> Manifestations might include the targeting of the state of Israel, conceived as a Jewish collectivity. However, criticism of Israel similar to that levelled against any other country cannot be regarded as antisemitic. Antisemitism frequently charges Jews with conspiring to harm humanity, and it is often used to blame Jews for 'why things go wrong'. It is expressed in speech, writing, visual forms and action, and employs sinister stereotypes and negative character traits. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 1]

Eleven specific examples, of which seven mention Israel:

> Accusing the Jews as a people, or Israel as a state, of inventing or exaggerating the Holocaust.

> Accusing Jewish citizens of being more loyal to Israel, or to the alleged priorities of Jews worldwide, than to the interests of their own nations.

> Denying the Jewish people their right to self-determination, e.g., by claiming that the existence of a State of Israel is a racist endeavor.

> Applying double standards by requiring of it a behavior not expected or demanded of any other democratic nation.

>  Using the symbols and images associated with classic antisemitism (e.g., claims of Jews killing Jesus or blood libel) to characterize Israel or Israelis.

> Drawing comparisons of contemporary Israeli policy to that of the Nazis.

> Holding Jews collectively responsible for actions of the state of Israel. [@ihra_definition_2016, p. 2]

# The main drivers of contemporary antisemitism in liberal democracies

Similarities and differences with regard to earlier periods in history.

Antisemitism 

Islamism and other Muslim supremacist movements.

# Zionism, antizionism, and antisemitism

While the establishment of the state of Israel did not introduce a fundamental break into the historical development of antisemitism, it did ... especially in the Muslim world. ...

From treating Jews as a despised subordinate minority, dispersed throughout the world, to seeking to return them to their status as a subordinate minority, dispersed throughout the world ...

> the launch event for the 'Anti-Zionist Movement' ... featuring individuals such as David Miller, Rahmeh Aladwan, and Latifa Abouchakra, all of whom regularly post on social media about so-called 'Jewish supremacy' in control of the UK [@rosenberg_birmingham_2026]

'antizionism is a new form of Judeophobia only in the sense that what stands between the Jew haters and their enemy (the Jew) has changed', such that 'Israel is merely the new obstacle that stands between Judeophobes and the fulfilment of their unsubstantiated hatred against the Jews' [@fuchshuber_wilhelm_2019, p. 35].

It is my view that, while the existence of Israel has changed the material reality of the world in which Jews and antisemites live, it has not introduced a break in the historical development of antisemitism.

Hatred of Jews when they were dispersed throughout the diaspora is not discontinuous with hatred of Jews now that there is a nation in which they constitute the majority.

Indeed, it would require considerable mental gymnastics to argue that hating the Jews of Israel, and wanting Israel as a Jewish state to be wiped from the map, is not ...

> Classical antisemitism was anti-assimilationist: Jews were constructed as an alien minority incapable of belonging within European nation-states. Antizionism, by contrast, is structurally assimilationist: Jews may exist as individuals or as a religion, but Jewish nationhood and sovereignty are treated as illegitimate. Sovereignty becomes the offense. This inversion produces a systematic reclassification of the Jew—from infiltrating minority to colonizing majority—and generates a new set of cultural codings that are inverse to classical antisemitic constructions. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> Early texts from the 1920s by Alfred Rosenberg—later one of the central Nazi propagandists—framed the 'Zionist menace' as an invasion of Palestine, decades before Patrick Wolfe's formulation of settler-colonial 'invasion'.  Rosenberg applied classical antisemitic notions of Jewish 'conquest', found in 19th century antisemites such as Wilhelm Marr and Eduard Drumont, equally to Jewish presence in Germany and to Jewish settlement in Palestine. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

Inverse Cultural Categories: Antisemitism and Antizionism

| Antisemitism | Antizionism |
| ------------ | ----------- |
| Rejection of Jews as minority | Rejection of Jewish majority-state' |
| Non-white, oriental, semitic | White settler, European |
| Jews as communists, Judeo-Bolsheviks | Jews as fascists, ultra-nationalists| 
| Tends to reject token Jews | Invests heavily in token Jews |
| Logics of elimination, physical genocide | Assimilation, cultural genocide |
| Uses biological sciences as authorisation | Uses social sciences as authorization' |
| Jews as a race | 'Zionism\' as an ideology |
| Jews infiltrate European states | Jews colonize non-European peoples |
| Exaggerates Jewish distinctiveness| Erases Jewish distinctiveness |

| Common logics |
| :-----------: |
| Jews as liars, manipulative, deceptive |
| Jews aligned with power and domination |

[@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> In practice, nearly all definitions of antisemitism—IHRA, JDA, Nexus—presume that the task is to determine when antizionism 'crosses the line' into antisemitism. While this is often framed as distinguishing legitimate criticism from anti-Jewish bigotry, it effectively legitimizes antizionism by default as a form of criticism, recognizing only classical antisemitism as bigotry.

> The 'line crossing' is typically marked by echoes of historical antisemitism: media and government control, dual-loyalty accusations, or racial infiltration. Only when antizionism resembles these older patterns does it 'count' as bigotry. Meanwhile, the specific libels that define antizionism—colonizer, apartheid, genocide, white-coding—are cast as political or moral critique. 

> When the discourse turns to Israeli policy, or even Israel's right to exist, the framework shifts to concerns about 'double standards', or to Natan Sharansky's 'three D's' (delegitimization, demonization, and double standards).  Yet the core libels and symbolic structure of antizionism remain unnamed. There is a widespread assumption that 'antisemitic tropes' exist --- money, secret power, media control --- but not that there are distinctive antizionist tropes: white coloniser, Jewish supremacist, genocidaire. The same problems plague the category of 'political antisemitism', which does not specifically name antizionist libels but instead assumes that classical antisemitic tropes have simply been projected onto Israel. While such projection does sometimes occur, this framework fails to identify the specific ideological content of antizionist hate in the contemporary. In this way, the possibility that antizionism constitutes a distinct, contemporary formation of anti-Jewish bigotry is foreclosed in advance—by making antisemitism the generic category and classical antisemitism the normative model of hate. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> Antizionism is ... not classical antisemitism projected onto Israel but a distinct ideological and symbolic complex whose core nucleus consists of the colonizer, apartheid, and genocide libels. Recognizing this requires moving beyond the 'line-crossing' frameworks that anchor the analysis in classical antisemitism. [@louis-klein_antizionism_2026, n.p.]

> The genocide accusation against Israel is precisely aimed at delegitimizing the state. It is to single it out as a state different from others. The ... determination of genocide ... is not based on the application [of] the strict legal doctrinal requirement of proving the intent to destroy. The attempt to argue that statements by Netanyahu, Gallant and Herzog amount to evidence of that intent involves taking the words out of context [and] ignoring that all three are talking about Hamas ... . It is particularly grotesque given the genocidal intent of the Islamic Republic of Iran and its proxies, that Israel is accused of the very crime that it is the victim of. [@strawson_israel_2026, n.p.]

> antizionists stress a radical discontinuity between the two. They argue that, even if traces remain, antisemitism ended in the mid-twentieth century, coinciding with the end of the Holocaust and the defeat of Nazism. This view is accompanied often by the belief that Jews are today, fully and finally, not only assimilated into their host countries, but also share the same privileges as the most dominant sectors of those societies. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 12]

> antizionism subsumes the antisemitism(s) of the past just as 'modern' or 'classic' antisemitism contained within it earlier, premodern forms of anti-Judaic praxis. In this way, any implication that non-antisemitic antizionism is legitimate can be challenged. I am arguing, in other words, that antizionism can never be legitimate contemporary political speech in the way that anti-Zionism may have been in the nineteenth century through to the mid-twentieth century. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 13]

>  that which the demonological Jew once was in older forms of antisemitism, demonological Israel now is in contemporary antisemitic anti-Zionism: uniquely malevolent, full of blood lust, all-controlling, the hidden hand, tricksy, always acting in bad faith, the obstacle to a better, purer, more spiritual world, uniquely deserving of punishment, and so on. [@johnson_written_2016, parag. 1.3]

> The necessity of recognising the existence of the ideology of antizionism as connected to, but distinct from, the ideology of antisemitism is more than a matter of semantics. The ideology of antizionism represents a new and novel 'reconfiguration' of the Jewish question. Although the nature of its anti-Jewish nature remains constant, its particular form and content is intimately connected to the specificities of Jewish emancipation. As much as Jewish emancipation adopts new forms so too does the ideological form of the Jewish question alter. In content and form, the ideology of antisemitism emerged and crystallized precisely at the moment Jews finally gained emancipation within the nation-state, achieved national self-determination. Moreover, antizionism, like antisemitism, was itself a combination of the old and the new, of continuity and discontinuity. Israel is new to the world and so too is the response from, and shape of, the Jewish question. The ideology of antizionism is that response. What is called for is not the subsumption of antizionism beneath antisemitism.
What is needed is recognition of the ways in which the ideology of antizionism has incorporated the ideology of antisemitism. [@seymour_continuity_2019, p. 22]

Community Security Trust in the UK:

> There were 1766 antisemitic incidents that showed explicitly anti-Zionist motivation, comprising 48% of the annual total, compared to 43% in 2024 and 31% in 2023. It is noteworthy that, over the last three years, the proportion of antisemitic incidents that display ideological anti-Zionism alongside anti-Jewish language or targeting has grown by a larger proportion than incidents that simply
contain Israel-related discourse. CST recorded 462 instances of anti-Jewish hate wherein the terms 'Zionism' or 'Zionist' were used, often as euphemisms for 'Jewishness' and 'Jew', or in conjunction with other antisemitic sentiment, rising from 423 cases in 2024 and 271 in 2023. Meanwhile, on 387 occasions, Israel, Israelis, or Jewish people were equated with Nazi Germany or
the Nazis: a rise from 333 such examples in 2024 and 257 in 2023. [@cst_antisemitic_2026, p. 5]

# Antisemitism as a threat to Jews

# Antisemitism as a threat to social cohesion in liberal democracies
